topic: World History Topic: Causes & Effects of 20th-Century Wars
subject: history

items.q: Did the First World War happen only because Franz Ferdinand was shot?
items.correct: No - the assassination was the spark; underlying alliance systems, militarism, imperial rivalry and the July Crisis decisions turned it into a general war.
items.misconception: One assassination on its own caused the First World War.
items.random_wrong: The First World War began over a dispute about colonial trade in Africa alone.
items.why: IB essays must distinguish triggers from long-term causes; collapsing them into one event prevents real causal analysis.

items.q: Did the Treaty of Versailles make the Second World War inevitable?
items.correct: No - it created grievances, but two decades of choices intervened: the Depression, Hitler's decisions, the failure of collective security and appeasement; historians still debate the weighting.
items.misconception: Versailles alone automatically caused the Second World War.
items.random_wrong: The Treaty of Versailles was never actually signed.
items.why: Determinist shortcuts ignore contingency; examiners reward weighing Versailles against later decisions and conditions.

items.q: Are the effects of war only military and territorial?
items.correct: No - twentieth-century wars also transformed societies: women's roles, welfare states, decolonisation pressures, refugee movements and new international bodies.
items.misconception: The results of a war are just casualty figures and changed borders.
items.random_wrong: Wars have no effects once a peace treaty is signed.
items.why: The syllabus explicitly requires political, economic, social and territorial effects; narrow answers miss most of the marks.

items.q: Which event triggered the outbreak of the First World War?
items.correct: The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand in Sarajevo in June 1914
items.why: It's The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand in Sarajevo in June 1914. “The German invasion of Poland” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: A conflict mobilising a society's entire economy and population, blurring the line between home front and battlefield
items.misconception: A war fought on every continent at once
items.random_wrong: A war declared by every member of the United Nations
items.why: Correct answer: A conflict mobilising a society's entire economy and population, blurring the line between home front and battlefield. “A war fought on every continent at once” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which of these is a long-term cause of the Second World War?
items.correct: German resentment of the Treaty of Versailles
items.why: Correct answer: German resentment of the Treaty of Versailles. “The attack on Pearl Harbor” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which of these was an effect of the Second World War?
items.correct: The founding of the United Nations in 1945
items.misconception: The creation of the League of Nations
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The founding of the United Nations in 1945. “The creation of the League of Nations” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did the Second World War begin in Europe?
items.why: Exact answer: 1939 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
